metadata.title: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = the fixed z-component (what the MRI scanner reads); CRIMSON = the precessing azimuthal motion (the frequency the scanner listens for)
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no Hamiltonian diagonalization, no g-factor/QED aside, no pulse-sequence engineering. Mechanism only: spin in z-field keeps polar angle fixed while azimuth advances at Larmor frequency omega=gamma*B.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Every MRI scanner works by listening. Millions of proton spins inside you are circling at the same frequency — and the scanner picks up that exact rotation.

props.command: Why do proton spins precess at the frequency used by MRI?
props.segment: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI
props.runningText: tracking relative phase around the Bloch sphere…

card.copy: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI

beats.narration_text: Drop a proton into a magnetic field pointing up. The proton's spin arrow tilts at some angle — and then, instead of staying still, it starts to circle. Like a tilted top that won't fall.
The z-component stays exactly fixed the whole time. Only the horizontal part rotates. The spin is on a circle at constant latitude — precessing.
The Question: the z-component of the spin never changes. Yet the spin is clearly doing something — circling at a constant rate. What fixes that rate, and why does the z-component stay frozen?

card.copy: Lz is frozen. The azimuth rotates. What sets the rate, and why the split?

beats.narration_text: Naive picture: a field should simply pull the spin into alignment, all the way up or down. But without dissipation, even a classical magnetic moment precesses. The quantum question is what rotates when the spin has only two basis states.

The mechanism: the field makes each spin eigenstate advance its phase at a different rate. The up-state gains phase at one speed, the down-state at another. Their relative phase is the azimuth on the Bloch sphere — and that relative phase ticks forward at their difference.

The angular rate is the Larmor frequency: omega equals the magnitude of gamma times B. Gamma is the gyromagnetic ratio, including its sign convention, and B is the field strength. Double the field, and the frequency magnitude doubles.

Why does Lz stay fixed? The phase advance on each eigenstate doesn't change the probability of finding the spin up or down — only the relative phase between them changes. The polar angle is unchanged. Only the azimuth moves.

In a 1.5 tesla clinical MRI scanner, proton frequency is about 63.9 megahertz — nearly 64 million cycles per second. At 3 tesla, it doubles to about 127.7 megahertz. The scanner applies radiofrequency pulses near that resonance and detects the ensemble response.

props.headline: In a 1.5 Tesla clinical MRI scanner, protons precess at 63.9…

beats.scene_description: Interior of a clinical MRI scanner bore, circular magnets surrounding a patient table, showing the scale and field geometry

beats.narration_text: Your turn. A proton spin is tilted 60 degrees from z in a 1.5 tesla field. Its z expectation is hbar over two times cosine 60, or hbar over four, and it stays fixed. The azimuth cycles at about 63.9 megahertz while the transverse expectation rotates at that frequency.
beats.example_note: illustrative — numbers from the chapter's worked example at 1.5 T

beats.narration_text: Why does a proton spin circle in a magnetic field without ever aligning? Because the field advances the relative phase of the up and down eigenstates — moving the azimuth — while leaving the amplitudes, and therefore the z-projection, exactly unchanged.

card.copy: omega = gamma * B. The z-component is frozen. The azimuth circles.
